infobox.value: Robert Michael O'Hare Jr.  (1952-05-06)May 6, 1952  [Chicago, Illinois](/wiki/Chicago,_Illinois "Chicago, Illinois"), U.S.
September 28, 2012(2012-09-28) (aged 60)  [New York City, New York](/wiki/New_York_City,_New_York "New York City, New York"), U.S.

*[The Equalizer](/wiki/The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series) "The Equalizer (1985 TV series)")* | Alex | Episode: "[Wash Up](/wiki/The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series)_season_1#ep16 "The Equalizer (1985 TV series) season 1")"

*[LA Law](/wiki/LA_Law "LA Law")* | Dr. Michael Lattimer | 1 episode

(May 6, 1952 – September 28, 2012) was an American actor who performed on stage and television. He was best known for playing the lead role of space station Commander [Jeffrey Sinclair](/wiki/Jeffrey_Sinclair "Jeffrey Sinclair") in the first season of the science fiction television series *[Babylon 5](/wiki/Babylon_5 "Babylon 5")*.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American stage and television actor

For the British chef, see [Michael O'Hare (chef)](/wiki/Michael_O%27Hare_(chef) "Michael O'Hare (chef)").

| Michael O'Hare | |
| --- | --- |
| O'Hare as Jeffrey Sinclair in *[Babylon 5](/wiki/Babylon_5 "Babylon 5")* | |

| Died | September 28, 2012(2012-09-28) (aged 60)  [New York City, New York](/wiki/New_York_City,_New_York "New York City, New York"), U.S. |

**Robert Michael O'Hare Jr.** (May 6, 1952 – September 28, 2012) was an American actor who performed on stage and television. He was best known for playing the lead role of space station Commander [Jeffrey Sinclair](/wiki/Jeffrey_Sinclair "Jeffrey Sinclair") in the first season of the science fiction television series *[Babylon 5](/wiki/Babylon_5 "Babylon 5")*.

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_O%27Hare&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Robert Michael O'Hare Jr. was born in Chicago, Illinois and grew up in Chicago Heights. His father, Robert Michael O’Hare Sr., was of Irish descent while his mother, Sally O’Hare (née Crisanti) was of Italian descent. He attended Chicago's Mendel Catholic Preparatory High School,[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) where he played [football](/wiki/American_football "American football"), to defy his doctor who told him he would never be in athletics because of his asthma.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) He received several awards and scholarship offers based on his football ability and scholastic performance.[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Coming from a career military family,[[11]](#cite_note-11) he considered joining the [US Navy](/wiki/US_Navy "US Navy") or having a career in professional football but attended Harvard University, where he studied English literature[[6]](#cite_note-:0-6) and played on the [Harvard Crimson football team](/wiki/Harvard_Crimson_football "Harvard Crimson football").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) He joined the university's drama groups[[15]](#cite_note-15) and was a performer in “The Wrongway Inn”,[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17) the [Hasty Pudding Theatricals](/wiki/Hasty_Pudding_Theatricals "Hasty Pudding Theatricals")’ production for 1972.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) That same year, he went for an "acting tryout" to the [New York](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area") area, and was cast as Beef Saunders in [*Good News!*](/wiki/Good_News_(musical) "Good News (musical)") at [Goodspeed Musicals](/wiki/Goodspeed_Musicals "Goodspeed Musicals").[[20]](#cite_note-20) He was a member of the [Harvard Glee Club](/wiki/Harvard_Glee_Club "Harvard Glee Club") during its 1973 World Tour.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) He left Harvard in 1974 to study at the [Juilliard School](/wiki/Juilliard_School "Juilliard School") of Drama. He later took lessons from [Sanford Meisner](/wiki/Sanford_Meisner "Sanford Meisner") in the mid-1980s.[[23]](#cite_note-:1-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_O%27Hare&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

O'Hare appeared in a number of theatrical productions on Broadway and in regional theaters, including an acclaimed revival of Shaw's *[Man and Superman](/wiki/Man_and_Superman "Man and Superman")*[[25]](#cite_note-25) with [Philip Bosco](/wiki/Philip_Bosco "Philip Bosco"); in the role of Col. Jessup in the original stage version of *[A Few Good Men](/wiki/A_Few_Good_Men_(play) "A Few Good Men (play)");*[[26]](#cite_note-26) as Captain Solyony in [Chekhov](/wiki/Anton_Chekhov "Anton Chekhov")’s [*Three Sisters*](/wiki/Three_Sisters_(play) "Three Sisters (play)").[[27]](#cite_note-27) Other notable roles included Alfred in a 1986 stage revival of *[Little Murders](/wiki/Little_Murders "Little Murders")*;[[23]](#cite_note-:1-23)[[28]](#cite_note-28) Jake in *[A Lie of the Mind](/wiki/A_Lie_of_the_Mind "A Lie of the Mind")*[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) and John in *[Lips Together, Teeth Apart](/wiki/Lips_Together,_Teeth_Apart "Lips Together, Teeth Apart")*.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

He was the first white actor nominated by the African-American theater community of New York for the [AUDELCO Award](/wiki/AUDELCO "AUDELCO") for Best Actor, for his performance as Captain Jaap van Tonder[[33]](#cite_note-33) in Michael Picardie's play *Shades of Brown*, about [apartheid](/wiki/Apartheid "Apartheid") in South Africa.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)

He co-starred in the biographical TV movie *Marciano*[[36]](#cite_note-36) and an unsold TV pilot *Keefer* with [William Conrad](/wiki/William_Conrad "William Conrad") in the late 1970s. In the 1980s, he appeared mainly as guest on a range of television shows, including *[Trapper John, M.D.](/wiki/Trapper_John,_M.D. "Trapper John, M.D.")*; *[T.J. Hooker](/wiki/T.J._Hooker "T.J. Hooker")*; *[Kate & Allie](/wiki/Kate_%26_Allie "Kate & Allie")*; *[The Equalizer](/wiki/The_Equalizer_(1985_TV_series) "The Equalizer (1985 TV series)")*; *[Tales from the Darkside](/wiki/Tales_from_the_Darkside "Tales from the Darkside")*; and *[Rage of Angels](/wiki/Rage_of_Angels "Rage of Angels"): The Story Continues*.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

He was the lead character in Michael Lengsfield's [CINE](/wiki/CINE "CINE")-award-winning short film *[Short Term Bonds](/w/index.php?title=Short_Term_Bonds&action=edit&redlink=1 "Short Term Bonds (page does not exist)")* in 1988,[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) screened at the 1989 [Sundance festival](/wiki/Sundance_Film_Festival "Sundance Film Festival").

In 1992, he was cast in the lead role of Commander [Jeffrey Sinclair](/wiki/Jeffrey_Sinclair "Jeffrey Sinclair") in the science fiction television series *[Babylon 5](/wiki/Babylon_5 "Babylon 5")*. He appeared in the pilot and throughout the show's first season in 1994. He left the cast for mental health reasons that were not disclosed at the time, but made guest appearances in the second and third seasons.

Subsequently, he had one guest role on *[The Cosby Mysteries](/wiki/The_Cosby_Mysteries "The Cosby Mysteries")* and appeared twice on *[Law & Order](/wiki/Law_%26_Order "Law & Order")*, the last time in 2000. He did some voiceover work for commercials and read a radio adaptation of the science fiction novella *[Think Like a Dinosaur](/wiki/Think_Like_a_Dinosaur "Think Like a Dinosaur")* for [Seeing Ear Theater](/wiki/Seeing_Ear_Theater "Seeing Ear Theater").[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)

After 2000, he retired from acting and rarely made public appearances.

## Illness and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Michael_O%27Hare&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Illness and death")]

*Babylon 5* creator [J. Michael Straczynski](/wiki/J._Michael_Straczynski "J. Michael Straczynski") revealed after O'Hare's death that the actor has had severe mental illness.[[43]](#cite_note-jms-phoenix-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-Blastr-Moving-Story-45) During the filming of the first season of *Babylon 5*, O'Hare began having [paranoid delusions](/wiki/Paranoid_delusion "Paranoid delusion"), and, halfway through, his [hallucinations](/wiki/Hallucination "Hallucination") worsened. It became increasingly difficult for O'Hare to continue working, his behavior was becoming increasingly erratic, and he was often at odds with his colleagues. Straczynski offered to suspend production for several months to accommodate treatment; O'Hare feared that a hiatus would put the series at risk and he did not want to jeopardize others' jobs. Straczynski agreed to keep O'Hare's condition secret to protect his career and O'Hare agreed to complete the first season but would be written out of the second season so that he could seek treatment. His departure from the cast was announced without explanation, except that it was mutual and amicable.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)

His treatments were only partially successful. He reappeared in a cameo appearance early in season two ("[The Coming of Shadows](/wiki/The_Coming_of_Shadows "The Coming of Shadows")") and returned in season three for a two-part episode ("[War Without End](/wiki/War_Without_End_(Babylon_5) "War Without End (Babylon 5)")") which closed his [character's story arc](/wiki/Character_arc "Character arc"). At that time, Straczynski promised O'Hare to keep his condition secret "to my grave". O'Hare told him to instead "keep the secret to *my* grave", arguing that fans deserved to eventually learn the real reason for his departure, and that his experience could raise awareness and understanding for people with mental illness. He made no further appearances on *Babylon 5* but continued to support the show and appeared at conventions and signing events until his retirement from public appearances in 2000.

On September 28, 2012, Straczynski posted that O'Hare had had a heart attack in New York City five days earlier and had remained in a coma until his death that day.[[48]](#cite_note-48) Eight months later, Straczynski revealed the circumstances of O'Hare's departure from *Babylon 5* at a presentation about the series at the [Phoenix Comicon](/wiki/Phoenix_Comicon "Phoenix Comicon").[[43]](#cite_note-jms-phoenix-43)[[45]](#cite_note-Blastr-Moving-Story-45)
